final_answer: The worst-case message complexity of the Bully leader-election algorithm is **O(N^2)**. In the worst case, an election initiator sends **Election** messages to all higher-ID processes, and if higher-ID processes respond they each start their own election, creating a cascading set of elections whose total number of messages grows on the order of **N^2** (the provided evidence does not include an exact worst-case closed-form count).

final_answer: In the worst case, Bully sends Θ(n^2) messages. Reason: when a leader failure is detected, a process sends an “Election” to every higher-ID process it can reach; in the worst-case (e.g., failures/activations cause many overlapping election triggers before the highest surviving node settles the leadership), the total number of such sends across all triggers sums to a quadratic bound, so the worst-case message complexity is O(n^2).

final_answer:
Here is a side-by-side comparison focused on the consensus goal, leadership handling, and majority/quorum requirement:

| Aspect | Paxos | Raft |
|---|---|---|
| **Consensus goal** | Paxos lets a distributed group agree on a single value despite node failures, provided a majority remains available [E1]. Its safety property is that only one value is decided [E3]. | Raft is a consensus protocol that decomposes consensus into leader election, log replication, and safety [E5]. It is also a consensus protocol for agreeing despite failures, with progress requiring a majority [E8]. |
| **Leadership handling** | Basic Paxos has no stable leader [E4]. In the two phases, a Proposer asks Acceptors for promises in Phase 1, then broadcasts a value in Phase 2 after a majority of promises [E2]. Multi-Paxos adds a stable leader to amortize the Prepare phase across multiple decisions [E4]. | Raft divides time into terms; in each term, at most one leader is elected [E6]. The leader appends client requests to its log and replicates entries to followers [E6]. |
| **Majority / quorum requirement** | Paxos requires a majority of nodes to be available for progress [E1, E8]. The quorum size is (N/2) + 1 for N nodes [E9]. Paxos’s Phase 2 proceeds after a majority of promises [E2], which is the quorum condition tied to deciding values safely [E1, E3, E9]. | Raft requires a majority of nodes to be available for progress [E8]. An entry is committed when it is replicated to a majority of nodes [E7]. The quorum size is (N/2) + 1 for N nodes [E9]. The leader’s commit rule also requires that the entry be stored on a majority and that at least one new entry from the leader’s term is on a majority [E10]. |

**Synthesis**
Both protocols rely on majority quorums of size ⌊N/2⌋+1 (equivalently, (N/2)+1 as stated in the evidence) for correctness/progress conditions [E8, E9]. Paxos centers on proposer/acceptor phases and can run without a stable leader, though Multi-Paxos introduces one to reduce repeated Prepare overhead [E2, E4]. Raft makes leadership explicit via term-based leader election and uses the leader to drive log replication and commitment, with commit occurring only after majority replication [E5, E6, E7, E10].

final_answer:
**Cited evidence**
[E1] "Paxos runs in two phases: Phase 1 (Prepare/Promise), where a Proposer requests acceptance rights from Acceptors."
[E2] "Phase 2 (Accept/Accepted) is where the Proposer broadcasts a value after it has a majority of promises."
[E3] "5.1 Phase 1. 5.1.1 Phase 1a: Prepare; 5.1.2 Phase 1b: Promise · 5.2 Phase 2. 5.2.1 Phase 2a: Accept; 5.2.2 Phase 2b: Accepted"

**Answer**
The two primary Paxos phases are **Phase 1: Prepare/Promise** and **Phase 2: Accept/Accepted**. Phase 1 is the leader-election / proposal-preparation step in which a proposer seeks promises or acceptance rights from acceptors [E1, E3]. Phase 2 is the consensus step in which the proposer sends the value to be accepted after obtaining a majority of promises [E2, E3].
